data.title: Watch live: RFK Jr. to press medical schools on highlighting nutrition
data.description:
Health and Human Services (HHS) Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. will speak with reporters Thursday morning about pushing medical schools to teach more about nutrition as part of the Trump administration’s “Make America Healthy Again” agenda.

The press conference comes weeks after Kennedy unveiled new dietary guidelines and an upside-down food pyramid that places more importance on vegetables, fruits, proteins, dairy and healthy fats while deemphasizing whole grains at the bottom. 

The health chief has also notably focused much of his policy initiatives on healthier diets and exercise instead of more medication.

Education Secretary Linda McMahon will join Kennedy.

The event is scheduled to begin at 11 a.m. EST.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: Democrats should nominate Joe Scarborough in 2028
data.description:
It’s not too early to start talking about the 2028 presidential election. An early December Emerson College poll had California Gov. Gavin Newsom (D) leading the field for the Democratic nomination at 36 percent, followed by former Transportation Secretary Pete Buttigieg at 16 percent, Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (D-N.Y.) at 13 percent and former Vice President Kamala Harris at 9 percent. 

There is a long list of others speculated to be strong candidates for the nomination. This includes governors such as Josh Shapiro of Pennsylvania, JB Pritzker of Illinois, Wes Moore of Maryland, Gretchen Whitmer of Michigan and Andy Beshear of Kentucky, as well as Sen. Elissa Slotkin (D-Mich.) and sports and political commentator Stephen A. Smith.

But there is one name that should be added to this list: Joe Scarborough.

Scarborough is the closest thing to a Goldilocks presidential candidate for the Democrats — not too liberal or “woke” to scare off moderate Democrats and independents, and not too conservative to alienate the liberal base.

Scarborough is unique in that he is currently an outsider of Washington politics, but for years he was an insider as a Republican congressman from Florida. And as we have learned from 2024, national polls are meaningless. The key is to have a candidate who can win the swing states of Michigan, Wisconsin, Pennsylvania, Arizona, North Carolina, Georgia and Nevada. 

There is a huge debate in the Democratic Party as to whether to run a staunch liberal or progressive for president, as opposed to a more moderate candidate. I have been a staunch liberal for my entire life, but I believe the Democratic party needs to be practical when nominating its presidential candidate in 2028. 

An Emerson College poll released in late December revealed that a hypothetical generic moderate Democratic presidential candidate would beat a MAGA Republican presidential candidate, 47 percent to 38 percent. 

Scarborough was a conservative Republican representing Florida’s panhandle from 1995 to 2001. A lawyer, he served on the Judiciary, Armed Services, Oversight and National Security committees. He retired from Congress in 2001 and in 2003 became the host of MSNBC’s “Scarborough Country.” His MS NOW show, “Morning Joe,” co-hosted by his wife Mika Brzezinski, began in 2007.

Scarborough officially left the Republican Party in 2017 to become an independent. But he has years of credibility with liberals who have watched him for the last decade defending Joe Biden, Hillary Clinton and other prominent Democratic leaders.

He also has been relentless in his criticism of President Trump. His scathing criticism persisted, even though he and his wife had a controversial meeting with Trump at Mar-a-Lago after the 2024 election. Many Democrats criticized Scarborough for meeting with Trump, but they need to get over it, especially since he has continued his persistent and scathing criticism of the current Trump administration. 

The fact that Scarborough is an independent, not a Democrat, should not matter. After all, Sen. Bernie Sanders (I-Vt.) had a lot of Democratic support in his run for president in 2020, even though he is an independent.

With his time in Congress, Scarborough checks the box of having government experience and knowledge of how Washington operates. With his time as a media personality on a liberal-oriented cable television network, Scarborough checks the box of having a great deal of name recognition with the liberal base. His liberal viewers know him, are comfortable with him, and agree with most of his positions.

On the air, he has expressed his support for many positions that liberals would agree with, including being pro-choice on abortion, supportive of reasonable gun control measures, pro-civil rights and social justice, pro-science and education, pro-environment, pro-free speech, pro-Democracy and critical of the Trump administration’s harsh immigration enforcement efforts.

Scarborough frequently cites the Gospel of Matthew and its emphasis on serving the poor and the needy. He also comes across as a regular guy who can talk about South Park and SEC football as comfortably as he can discuss complex policy issues.

Democrats should not be too reliant on their sugar high of Zohran Mamdani’s convincing win in the New York Mayoral race. Just because a Democratic Socialist won a citywide election in a heavily left-leaning city doesn’t mean that his far-left approach to government will resonate in swing states during a national presidential election. What plays in Prospect Heights will not play in Peoria.

There are many progressive Democrats who don’t want a moderate to be their party’s presidential candidate, arguing that “Liberal Lite” won’t cut it. But based on the 2024 presidential election, liberals would be less likely to stay home in 2028, even if the Democratic nominee is a moderate.

They are more likely to hold their nose and vote for a moderate Democratic candidate, realizing that the perfect shouldn’t outweigh the good and that a moderate would be better than a continuation of the Trump MAGA regime — most likely in the form of a JD Vance presidency.

Scarborough should be on the radar for the Democratic presidential nomination in 2028. Morning Joe would translate well to morning in America.

Larry Atkins teaches Journalism at Temple University and Arcadia University and Social and Ethical Issues in Sports at Arcadia. He is the author of “Skewed: A Critical Thinker’s Guide to Media Bias” and “Foul or Fair? Ethical and Social Issues in Sports.”

data.title: Pence: Trump 'made the right call' on Iran
data.description:
Former Vice President Mike Pence, who served under President Trump in his first term, said Wednesday that Trump “made the right call” on the U.S. operation against Iran.

Pence credited the Trump team, which he has sometimes broken with on tariffs and other issues, for seeking to find a diplomatic solution after strikes last summer aimed at eliminating Iran’s ability to develop nuclear weapons.

“But having struck with Operation Midnight Hammer, you witnessed the administration, again, engage diplomatically, not only on a nuclear program, but also on saying to Iran, ‘You have to end this support for malign influence and terrorism across the region,’ ‘You have to accept limitations on your ballistic missile and long-range missile program,’” Pence told CNN’s Kaitlan Collins on “The Source,” referencing the summer 2025 U.S. attack on Iranian enrichment plants.

“They refused to do that. And I think, I think at the end of the day, the president, history will record that he made the right call. The opportunity presented itself, and the president made the decision to unleash our armed forces,” he added.

The U.S. and Israel launched strikes late last week against Iran following weeks of American-Iranian tensions. The strikes killed Iran’s supreme leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, and rapidly drove up hostility in the region, with Iran retaliating via attacks against Israel and Gulf states.

The president’s decision to order strikes against Iran and other key locations was mostly supported by Republican lawmakers, though there have been some qualms about the potential for ground troops to be used, something Trump has not ruled out.

On Wednesday, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth went after media outlets over highlighting the deaths of six U.S. service members who died due to an Iranian drone strike in Kuwait in the American conflict with Iran.

The Defense secretary alleged that the press was trying to “make the president look bad” in noting the attack that targeted a reported triple-wide trailer that was U.S. military office space.

“Sure, Iran will still be able to shoot some missiles and still be able to launch one way attack drones at civilian targets, and their proxies will attempt to attack our embassies, bases and soft targets. They are terrorists, after all, and they need to target civilians because they can’t fight toe-to-toe,” Hegseth said at the Pentagon.

data.title: Iran calls for 'Trump's blood,' warning US will 'bitterly regret' sinking warship
data.description:
Iranian leaders are calling for President Trump’s “blood” after the U.S. and Israel launched an attack on Tehran over the weekend, vowing to take action over the “atrocity.”

Ayatollah Abdollah Javadi Amoli said Iran was looking to shed “Trump’s blood” on state television, according to The Associated Press. 

American forces have led assaults on land and water with assaults resulting in more than 1,000 Iranian casualties, per the AP. 

“The U.S. has perpetrated an atrocity at sea, 2,000 miles away from Iran’s shores. Frigate Dena, a guest of India’s Navy carrying almost 130 sailors, was struck in international waters without warning,” Iran’s Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi wrote in a post on social platform X.

“Mark my words: The U.S. will come to bitterly regret [the] precedent it has set,” he added.

On Wednesday, a U.S. Navy submarine sunk an Iranian warship in international waters, marking the first use of an underwater torpedo attack on an adversary ship since World War II.  The vessel was carrying around 130 Iranian soldiers. Sri Lankan authorities said 32 individuals were rescued while 87 people were pronounced deceased, according to the news wire.

Crew members on board were returning from an exercise led by the Indian government, which the U.S. also participated in. Casualties from the U.S. naval attack in international waters follow a strike on an Iranian school for girls in the city of Minab. 

Iranian officials say both bombings are considered illegal by international standards. 

Araghchi noted in a separate post on X that graves are being dug for more than 160 “innocent young girls” who were killed in the US-Israeli bombing of a primary school. 

“Their bodies were torn to shreds,” he wrote. “This is how ‘rescue’ promised by Mr. Trump looks in reality.”

“From Gaza to Minab, innocents murdered in cold blood,” the foreign minister added.

So far, Iran has retaliated to U.S. strikes with drone attacks on the U.S. Embassies in Riyadh, Saudi Arabia, and Kuwait. The Middle East nation has also launched counterattacks on other nations in the region, widening fears of a broader conflict.

One strike on a civilian port in Kuwait killed six U.S. service members.

Several others were wounded in another attack on the Al Dhafra Air Base in the United Arab Emirates, where U.S. soldiers are stationed, according to the AP. 

Despite the threats, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and Trump have defended the ongoing military operation in Iran.

data.title: 52 percent say US shouldn't have taken military action against Iran: Survey
data.description:
Just over half of registered voters say they the U.S. should not have taken military action against Iran, according to a new NBC News poll released Wednesday.

The outlet found 52 percent who say they are against the military action compared to 41 percent who say the U.S. should have taken action. Another 7 percent say they are not sure.

NBC News also found that 54 percent of voters disapprove of President Trump’s handling of the conflict, versus 41 percent, again, who approve. Five percent say they are unsure or do not have an opinion.

Differing opinions toward the military operation fell largely along party lines. Democrats and independents, at 89 percent and 58 percent, respectively, say the U.S. should not have attacked Iran.

The outlet found that 77 percent of Republicans back the strikes compared to 15 percent who did not support them. Self-identified MAGA-aligned Republicans overwhelmingly support the strikes at 90 percent, with 5 percent who disagree. Over half of non-MAGA-aligned Republicans support the military operation, with 36 percent who say they are against them.

NBC News also reported that two-thirds of voters under 35, along with 53 percent of respondents between ages 35 and 49, do not support the strikes. Fifty-two percent of those between 50 and 64 back the strikes. Opinion on the strikes was split among respondents 65 years and older.

Republican pollster Bill McInturff of Public Opinion Strategies told NBC News that support for the current military action is at a “lower level of support than in most of the major military action that we’ve seen.”

“Attitudes shift in this country based on results,” he added. “Things could get worse if the results are worse, and things can certainly get better if there’s a stable, better outcome here.”

NBC News stated that its polling from early 2002 to early 2003 showed that majorities of Americans supported U.S. military action in Iraq and backed Saddam Hussein’s removal from power.

Eighty-seven percent of Americans supported former President George W. Bush’s handling of the war on terror a month after the Sept. 11, 2001, attacks and during the earliest strikes in Afghanistan, the outlet observed.

Trump said early in the operation on Monday that he thought the polling for the military operation was “very good, but I don’t care about polling.”

“I don’t think the polling is low,” he told the New York Post. “Look, whether polling is low or not, I think the polling is probably fine. But it’s not a question of polling. You cannot let Iran, who’s a nation that has been run by crazy people, have a nuclear weapon.”

The NBC News survey was conducted Feb. 27 to March 3 and included 1,000 respondents. The margin of error is 3.1 percentage points.

data.title: In the West Bank, Israel is brazenly defying Trump
data.description:
When it comes to Israel and the Palestinians, President Trump has focused his diplomatic efforts on Gaza — understandably so. More than 60 percent of Gaza’s housing stock has been destroyed after three and a half years of conflict there. The latest reporting indicates that 1.6 million people are facing acute food insecurity there.

But no Gaza strategy can succeed while ignoring the West Bank, where the Israeli government, in a rush to annex the territory, is brazenly defying Trump. In fact, if the Trump administration doesn’t act soon, Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu will have outmaneuvered Trump and derailed America’s efforts in Gaza. 

The link between Gaza progress and halting West Bank annexation is not new. America’s regional allies raised it during ceasefire talks, prompting Trump to tell Netanyahu privately (and later to reporters) that he would not allow Israel to annex the West Bank. Yet on the ground, annexation has sped up to a shocking degree. 

For decades, the U.S. has tolerated some level of settlement expansion, knowing that it carries with it the gradual loss of Palestinians’ access to territory and a political calculus for Israeli leaders that becomes increasingly stacked against any Israeli withdrawal. It has sometimes been called “creeping annexation.” But what’s happening now is no longer “creeping.” It is annexation, plain and simple. 

The clearest example is the E1 corridor east of Jerusalem. Building there would effectively sever the northern and southern West Bank and foreclose a viable Palestinian state. For decades, Israeli settlers have pushed for it to be built, developing plans for housing and massive highways. But successive Israeli governments have also heard from Israel’s European allies and from U.S. administrations of both parties that it must not happen.

Former U.S. Ambassador to Israel Tom Nides recounted that when he had to pick his battles and not oppose every Israeli settlement move, he decided to go “full bore on E1” because allowing the Israeli government to build it would be a “disaster.”

But Mike Huckabee is now ensconced in Nides’s old job — and Trump’s admonition apparently carries little weight, because Israel is now moving forward on this very plan. In January, Israel started accepting bids for the construction of 3,401 housing units, notifying landowners that their land was being seized and that work on the new highways would begin within 45 days.

Other major projects are also moving fast: The settlement of Adam is set for 6,000 units, with $39 million in new infrastructure funding, and 9,000 apartments intended for Orthodox Jews. These are moving forward smack in the middle of a densely populated Palestinian area between central Jerusalem and Ramallah. This list could easily go on and on.  

American foreign policy-makers have never witnessed anything like the fundamental legal changes that Netanyahu is now making to the West Bank. When Israel conquered the West Bank in 1967, international law mandated that it be ruled as an occupation, in which the military is in charge — not Israeli civilian authorities. When the Palestinian Authority started ruling parts of the West Bank in the 1990s, Israel ceded control in those areas. This legal framework recognized that Israeli rule was not a fait accompli but rather a temporary arrangement that would last until a diplomatic solution could be agreed upon. 

Now, in a series of audacious executive actions, Netanyahu’s government is flipping this legal framework on its head. In new cabinet decisions, Israel is no longer treated as an occupying power. The Israeli military is stripped of responsibilities related to its role as a military government, and powers provided to the Palestinian Authority under the Oslo Accords are dismantled. 

Taken together, the rapid integration of the West Bank into Israel’s legal regime and the historic pace of settlement construction is annexation. It is Netanyahu ignoring Trump’s admonition.   

Some will argue that Trump is instinctually more sympathetic to Israeli sovereignty claims than his predecessors, and that he may ultimately bless Netanyahu’s moves. But that carries a cost. Consider the prospect of Israel normalizing relations with Saudi Arabia — one of Trump’s signature foreign policy goals. It is explicitly contingent on a credible pathway to Palestinian statehood. Annexation kills that prospect. If Netanyahu succeeds, Trump doesn’t win. Rather, he loses one of the most consequential diplomatic opportunities of his presidency.

The downsides go beyond a missed peace deal. By accelerating annexation, Netanyahu is returning to the same political playbook that he has relied on for his entire career. He creates facts on the ground, avoids meaningful negotiations, and enables extremists like Hamas to rise. This is how he helped tie the Oslo Peace Process up in knots until it collapsed under its own weight. And this is how he created an illusion of stability vis-a-vis Gaza, until that collapsed in the horrors of October 7, 2023.  

We don’t have to let this history repeat. President Trump knows how to put pressure on Netanyahu. The question is whether Trump will recognize that the West Bank is where his Gaza strategy will be won or lost — before Netanyahu makes that choice for him. A ceasefire built atop annexation will not hold. When it collapses, the cost will once again be counted in lives. And when that happens, the responsibility will fall squarely on Trump for letting Netanyahu outfox him.

Lior Amihai is executive director of the Israeli Peace Now movement. Hadar Susskind is the president and CEO of New Jewish Narrative.

data.title: Trump backs former federal prosecutor for Daines Senate seat
data.description:
President Trump on Wednesday endorsed former federal prosecutor Kurt Alme to succeed Sen. Steve Daines (R-Mont.), who announced the same day he will not run for reelection in November.

“A Highly Respected Criminal Prosecutor in my Administration, and distinguished Harvard Law School Graduate, Kurt knows the Wisdom and Courage it takes to ensure LAW AND ORDER, advocate for our Heroes in Law Enforcement, and strongly support our Military and Veterans,” Trump wrote on Truth Social.

“As your next Senator, Kurt will fight tirelessly to Grow our Economy, Cut Taxes and Regulations, Champion our Farmers and Ranchers, Promote MADE IN THE U.S.A., Unleash American Energy DOMINANCE, Keep our Border SECURE, Stop Migrant Crime, and Protect our always under siege Second Amendment,” the president continued. “Kurt Alme is an exceptional person, and will do a fantastic job as your next United States Senator from the Great State of Montana — HE WILL NOT LET YOU DOWN!”

Trump noted that Daines “did a job like few others are capable of doing” and chose to “‘pass the torch’ to Kurt Alme, my TRUMP 45 and TRUMP 47 U.S. Attorney.” He added that Alme is “exceptional” and that Montana Gov. Greg Gianforte (R) and Sen. Tim Sheehy (R-Mont.) have also put their support behind him.

The president’s endorsement nearly one hour after the closing deadline to file to run in Montana.

Daines recommended Alme for the U.S. attorney role in Montana to Trump, according to the U.S. attorney’s office. He was unanimously confirmed in September 2017 and served until December 2020. He has since served as Gianforte’s state budget director and recently served as the National Christian Foundation’s executive vice president.

“We have made real progress under President Trump’s policies — prioritizing public safety, supporting law enforcement, and delivering results for Montanans,” Alme said in a statement to the Daily Montanan. “We need proven doers in Washington who will stand up for our values without apology. I’m running for Senate to be a relentless advocate for Montana and to help President Trump put America First.”

Daines said in a video shared on social platform X that he decided not to run again “after much careful thought.”

“Prior to public service, I spent 28 years in the private sector,” he said. “While I found those years very rewarding, they didn’t compare to the fulfillment I found in public service. I’m energized, I’m encouraged and I’m ready for whatever comes next.”

The Montana Republican joins several lawmakers choosing to forgo reelection bids in this year’s midterms. GOP lawmakers in particular have faced several political challenges ranging from concerns over affordability to Trump’s dropping approval ratings.

Daines’ announcement to not run again comes the day after Rep. Ryan Zinke (R-Mont.) announced he was retiring from Congress. Several surgeries that Zinke has “quietly undergone” since January 2023 prevented him from taking part in House votes, he said Tuesday, adding that he faces more surgeries in the future.

Zinke wrote in a statement that he chose to step away to allow for “full-time representation in Congress” and avoid the risk of “uncertain absence and missed votes.”

He and Trump endorsed Montana radio host Aaron Flint to succeed him.

data.title: Live updates: War powers debate shifts to House
data.description:
The House is expected to take up a vote on a war powers resolution that would curb any further U.S. military action in Iran, and Democrats are scrambling to bolster support for it.

Senate Republicans voted overwhelmingly against the upper chamber’s resolution seeking to rein in President Trump Wednesday, though several members are voicing concerns about the lack of a clear game plan to end the conflict. The House’s version is also expected to fail.

In Iran, an ayatollah has called for the “shedding” of blood from Israelis and Trump.

The Pentagon‘s No. 3 official, Elbridge Colby, will also face House questioning on the administrations National Defense Strategy, days after he testified before the Senate.

Meanwhile, attention remains fixed on Texas, where Sen. John Cornyn (R) is fighting for his political life in a Senate primary runoff, as GOP leaders fret over Democratic candidate James Talerico’s primary victory. Trump on Wednesday said he would endorse either Cornyn or state Attorney General Ken Paxton and then asked the unendorsed candidate to drop out of the race.

Trump on Thursday will hold executive time and an intelligence briefing, before hosting the Major League Soccer champions at the White House in the afternoon.

Follow live below.

data.title: Trump acts — others just watch as history is made
data.description:
From 1933 to 1945, Adolf Hitler and his psychotic regime turned Germany into a police state that imprisoned and massacred political opponents and conducted a vicious genocide that killed more than 13 million Jews, dissidents, Slavic peoples and other human beings considered socially or politically undesirable.

The regime plunged Europe and parts of Africa into a ruinous war, costing tens of millions more lives, including through the starvation of millions of prisoners of war. That war pulverized much of continental Europe and resulted in a nearly 45-year occupation by the Soviet Union, which visited two additional generations of oppression and misery on central and southeastern Europe.

But I guess it could have been worse. 

Or at least, that’s what I imagine most of the chattering classes must think, right? Any forceful intervention against any authoritarian regime is going to end badly. Regime change is always wrong. Any action results in a catastrophic butterfly effect that will assuredly wreck the future — Ray Bradbury “proved” that in 1952’s “A Sound of Thunder.” The only way to practice geopolitics is to do as little as possible. Make empty statements, engage in the occasional boycott and exist on the “right side of history” — surely, that is the path forward. 

Unfortunately, this logic does not work. For there is no proof that a foreordained “right side” exists.

History is made through action, not inaction. To be paralyzed by fear of making any mistakes or of imperfect results is to leave the door open for others to act. Brutal tyrants like Hitler, Lenin, Stalin, Putin and Khamenei have no compunction about taking action, and they don’t think much of Bradbury.

In the aftermath of the Cold War, thanks to the incompetence of Dick Cheney and Donald Rumsfeld, along with the election of Barack Obama, a vacuum developed. Into that vacuum rushed a gang of malevolent national leaders — leaders who eviscerated democracy, oppressed their own people, funded terrorism, attacked other sovereign nations and committed a laundry list of other crimes and outrages.

The response from the western democracies — the U.S. and its so-called allies — was empty rhetoric, appeasement and timidity. When Vladimir Putin first occupied parts of Ukraine, Obama and the Europeans responded by furiously wagging fingers and imposing weak sanctions. The Europeans continued to buy Russian hydrocarbons and allowed the completion of the Nord Stream 2 pipeline. Is it any wonder Putin thought he could get away with a full-scale invasion?

The menace from Iran is arguably greater than the one from Russia. Without Iranian finance and weapons, Hamas, Hezbollah and the Houthis would not exist as significant actors. These groups have destabilized and plunged into misery Lebanon and Yemen. Hamas oppresses and murders Palestinians who oppose their iron grip. All of them have repeatedly attacked Israeli, American and European interests and those of other nations in the region. Houthi attacks on shipping have been especially disastrous to the economy of Egypt, the world’s largest Arab nation with more than 100 million citizens. 

But even more dangerous are Iran’s nuclear ambitions. Despite years of sanctions interspersed with appeasement, Iran continued to build out its nuclear weapons infrastructure. To have several nuclear bombs in the control of a clique of religious fanatics who are beyond any public scrutiny is bad enough. But, given its stated position that Israel does not have a right to exist, the threat of nuclear attack cannot be automatically dismissed. 

Currently there are nine nations with nuclear weapons. Despite geopolitical frictions, none of them deny the right to exist of any of the others. The worst nuclear saber-rattling has been by Russia of late. However, at no time have the Russians taken any real steps to prepare any nuclear weapons for deployment, must less use. Russian rhetoric has clearly been about intimidation rather than a real threat. 

It is risky enough to tolerate a nuclear-armed Iran under the control of a gang of elderly religious clerics who fund and foment terrorism throughout their region. When you consider the recklessness of the Iranian regime, the chances for a nuclear miscalculation are exponentially higher than with any other nuclear power. And Israel, the chief target of its ire, is a small, densely populated nation that cannot afford to take chances with nuclear-armed missiles removed by less than 15 minutes‘ flight.  

Given the risks and the devastation that a nuclear exchange poses to the entire region, the destruction of the Iranian nuclear program on its own should be considered worth practically any price.

Trump’s Iran gambit is not without its risks. The failure of Cheney and Rumsfeld was their inability or unwillingness to think past the military conflict and to lead the U.S. into a ruinous, pointless “nation-building” quagmire.

Even a diminished radical Iranian regime and its terrorist proxies could continue to wreak havoc; if the current regime were deposed, any successor would have the challenge of ruling a fractious, ethnically diverse nation with a long history of internal conflict. 

Fortunately, Trump and his team seem to have learned from the mistakes of Iraq and Afghanistan. They are showing no interest in trying to govern or occupy Iran. Of course, there will be setbacks, costs and problems. There is no such thing as a free ride in geopolitics. But given the immense misery caused by the Iranian theocracy and its threat to the wider region, acting is a better policy than inaction. 

As for the chattering classes, maybe they should find a better book to read.  

Keith Naughton, a longtime Republican political consultant, is co-founder of Silent Majority Strategies, a public and regulatory affairs consulting firm, and a former Republican political campaign consultant in Pennsylvania.

data.title: Allam concedes to Foushee in tight North Carolina primary
data.description:
Rep. Valerie Foushee (D-N.C.) is expected to defend her seat in North Carolina’s 4th Congressional District after progressive challenger Nida Allam conceded on Wednesday.

Decision Desk HQ (DDHQ) has yet to call the narrow Democratic primary for the Durham-based seat.

“Though these were not the results we hoped for, I am proud of the movement we have built, the voices we have lifted up and the journey we have ahead,” Allam, a Durham County commisioner wrote in a statement.   

The two ran against each other in 2022 under different congressional lines, with Foushee winning the Democratic contest and Allam placing second. Foushee would go on to win the general election that year. 

With about 99 percent of the vote in as of Thursday morning, Foushee leads Allam by just 1,202 votes, per DDHQ.

“In the days ahead, the incumbent has a responsibility to half this district to earn their support,” Allam said in her Wednesday statement. “It should not take being challenged in a primary to take bold stances that voters support.”

Age and calls for generational change became a focal point in this contest. Allam, 32, said she felt compelled to run against Foushee, 69, again because the congresswoman wasn’t doing enough to fight back against the Trump administration.

“…To have our representative solely just put out a tweet or a strongly worded letter, when so many families were hurting, I couldn’t idly sit by and … look at my residents in good faith and say, ‘Well, you have to wait three years for help,’” Allam, who was backed by Sen. Bernie Sanders (I-Vt.) told The Hill in a recent interview, also slamming Foushee for taking money from corporate PACs. 

But defeating Foushee, a member of the Congressional Progressive Caucus, was no easy feat. Foushee enjoyed endorsements from former Gov. Roy Cooper (D), who’s running for North Carolina Senate this cycle, and current Gov. Josh Stein (D). 

Strategists suggested it would be tall task for Allam to topple Foushee given the two women had fewer policy differences than they did stylistic ones. 

“My constituents have clearly voiced to me that they want Congress to pass progressive policies to push this country forward, not simply replacing elected officials with younger ones without results,” Foushee told The Hill in a statement.  

“I respect our primary system but do not think this critical moment is the proper time to replace principled leadership with untested lip service,” Foushee added. 

Sophia Vento contributed to this report.
